title: Chow: Penang’s healthy surplus means no budget revision yet
text: Chow: Penang’s healthy surplus means no budget revision yet

title: Chow: Penang’s healthy surplus means no budget revision yet
text: GEORGE TOWN: Penang does not need to revise its state budget for now, despite economic uncertainty and rising costs, says Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow.

title: Chow: Penang’s healthy surplus means no budget revision yet
text: Chow said the state’s financial position remained healthy and that it was still recording a surplus.

title: Chow: Penang’s healthy surplus means no budget revision yet
text: “If we can save, there is no need to revise the budget,” he told reporters on Thursday (Apr 30).

title: Chow: Penang’s healthy surplus means no budget revision yet
text: Chow said the state is managing expenses, including rescheduling projects not yet awarded, in response to rising costs and spending demands.

title: Chow: Penang’s healthy surplus means no budget revision yet
text: He said projects already underway would continue as planned.

title: Chow: Penang’s healthy surplus means no budget revision yet
text: Chow added that some contractors had sought consideration over rising diesel prices.

title: Chow: Penang’s healthy surplus means no budget revision yet
text: “Penang Development Corporation (PDC) has decided to share the burden with contractors to help ease the situation,” he said.

title: Chow: Penang’s healthy surplus means no budget revision yet
text: He said there was no need for a supplementary budget at this point, as long as costs remained under control and state revenue collection met projections.

title: Inflation eases in 2025
text: Inflation eases in 2025

title: Inflation eases in 2025
text: KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia’s inflation moderated to 1.4% in 2025 as compared to 1.8% in the previous year, supported by the firmer ringgit, sustained household spending and initiatives by the government.

title: Inflation eases in 2025
text: According to the Statistics Department, government initiatives to distribute Ron95 petrol subsidies to Malaysians, to a certain extent, helped to curb further increases in Malaysia’s inflation.

title: Inflation eases in 2025
text: “This rate is within the inflation range projected by Bank Negara Malaysia and the Finance Ministry, between 1% and 2% for 2025,” it said in a statement regarding the analysis of consumer price index (CPI) 2025.

title: Inflation eases in 2025
text: Chief statistician Datuk Seri Dr Mohd Uzir Mahidin said the country’s inflation in 2025 was contributed by the inflation of housing, water, electricity, gas and other fuels (1.6%); health (1.2%); recreation, sports and culture (1.1%); transport (0.4%) and furnishings, household equipment and routine household maintenance (0.2%).

title: Inflation eases in 2025
text: He said personal care, social protection and miscellaneous goods and services recorded a higher increase at 4.4% compared to 2024’s 3%, followed by insurance and financial services (3.4%), restaurants and accommodation services (3.2%), education (2.3%), and food and beverages (2.1%).

title: Inflation eases in 2025
text: In contrast, he said information and communication and clothing and footwear recorded declines of negative 4.3% and negative 0.2% in 2025, respectively.

title: Inflation eases in 2025
text: “The highest inflation throughout 2025 was recorded in January 2025 at 1.7% and edged down to 1.1% in June 2025, contributed by a slower trend in the inflation of food and beverages, and housing, water, electricity, gas and other fuels.

title: Inflation eases in 2025
text: “Nevertheless, the inflation trend picked up in the second half of financial year 2025,” he further said.

title: Inflation eases in 2025
text: Mohd Uzir noted that the core inflation rose 2% in 2025 as compared to 1.8% in 2024, contributed by the personal care, social protection and miscellaneous goods and services (4.4%), food and beverages (3.4%), insurance and financial services (3.4%), and restaurant and accommodation services (3.2%).

title: Inflation eases in 2025
text: Meanwhile, he said most states registered slower inflation, with four states exceeding the national inflation rate of 1.4% in 2025, namely Johor (2%), Selangor (1.7%), Negri Sembilan (1.7%) and Melaka (1.5%).

title: Inflation eases in 2025
text: “Kelantan recorded the lowest increase at 0.3%,” he said.

title: Inflation eases in 2025
text: Mohd Uzir said that compared with inflation in other selected countries, the inflation rate in Asean countries ranged from negative 0.3% to 7.7% in 2025.

title: Inflation eases in 2025
text: “Laos recorded the highest inflation at 7.7%, while Brunei recorded the lowest inflation at negative 0.3%,” he added.

title: Inflation eases in 2025
text: — Bernama

title: More targeted price controls and subsidies needed for consumers
text: More targeted price controls and subsidies needed for consumers

title: More targeted price controls and subsidies needed for consumers
text: PETALING JAYA: Consumer groups are urging the government to introduce targeted price controls on essential goods alongside more focused subsidies to shield households from rising costs amid global economic uncertainty.

title: More targeted price controls and subsidies needed for consumers
text: They said temporary price caps could be extended beyond festive periods during times of volatility.

title: More targeted price controls and subsidies needed for consumers
text: Federation of Malaysian Consumers Associations chief executive officer Dr Saravanan Thambirajah said such controls should focus on essential items including rice, cooking oil, sugar, milk, eggs, fresh produce, flour, bread, chicken and fish.

title: More targeted price controls and subsidies needed for consumers
text: “Temporary and targeted controls for essential goods can be considered during periods of volatility and not just during festive seasons.

title: More targeted price controls and subsidies needed for consumers
text: “However, these must be supported by strong enforcement and anti-profiteering measures to ensure that the intended benefits reach consumers and are not undermined by market manipulation,” he said when contacted.

title: More targeted price controls and subsidies needed for consumers
text: Saravanan added that the government should also strengthen targeted subsidies and cash assistance to ensure support reaches vulnerable groups without distorting the market.

title: More targeted price controls and subsidies needed for consumers
text: “The government should enhance targeted subsidies and cash aid so that assistance reaches those who need it most, while improving supply chain transparency and addressing inefficiencies, including the role of intermediaries,” he said.

title: More targeted price controls and subsidies needed for consumers
text: Saravanan also stressed that cost-of-living pressures extend beyond food, calling for a broader approach that covers essential services such as healthcare, transport, utilities and financial services.

title: More targeted price controls and subsidies needed for consumers
text: He said a more coordinated mechanism is needed to address consumer issues, noting that responsibilities are currently spread across multiple ministries and agencies, which can lead to gaps in enforcement and confusion for the public.

title: More targeted price controls and subsidies needed for consumers
text: “A centralised platform or coordinating body would improve policy coherence, streamline responses and ensure more effective monitoring and intervention,” he said.

title: More targeted price controls and subsidies needed for consumers
text: Meanwhile, Consumers’ Assoc­iation of Penang president Mohi­deen Abdul Kader said Malaysia must continue implementing and closely monitoring price controls on essential food items as global uncertainties persist.

title: More targeted price controls and subsidies needed for consumers
text: He said part of the savings from reducing fuel subsidies could be redirected towards boosting food production, improving public transport and supporting those most in need.

title: More targeted price controls and subsidies needed for consumers
text: “With savings from fuel subsidy rationalisation, the government could also establish food banks and work with grassroots charitable organisations to reach those in desperate financial situations,” he said.

title: More targeted price controls and subsidies needed for consumers
text: Mohideen added that a portion of these savings could be channelled into food subsidies for the poorest groups to help cushion the impact of rising prices.

title: More targeted price controls and subsidies needed for consumers
text: He cautioned that maintaining artificially low fuel prices could have unintended consequences, including encouraging overconsumption and smuggling.

title: More targeted price controls and subsidies needed for consumers
text: “If RON95 prices continue to be pegged at RM1.99, there will be little incentive for people to drive less or switch to public transport,” he said.

title: More targeted price controls and subsidies needed for consumers
text: He also warned that rising food prices could push lower-income households towards cheaper, less nutritious diets, increasing the risk of malnutrition and diet-related diseases, while placing greater strain on public healthcare services.

title: More targeted price controls and subsidies needed for consumers
text: He added that prolonged inflationary pressures could also lead to a rise in loan defaults as borrowers struggle to meet their financial obligations.

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: JOHOR BARU: Many Indonesians are finding it difficult to get employment in Malaysia and would rather return home as they are worried about economic uncertainties, especially amid the Middle East conflict.

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: The Indonesian consul-general in Johor Baru, Sigit Suryantoro Widiyanto (pic), said although the ringgit is strengthening against the US dollar, the lack of job opportunities is one of the main reasons many are opting to leave under Malaysia’s Migrant Repatriation Programme 2.0, which ends today.

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: He added that records from the Consulate-General – which oversees Indonesian affairs in Johor, Melaka, Negri Sembilan and Pahang – show that 54,684 Indonesians registered for the programme in 2024 and 2025.

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: “We have yet to get the latest figures from the Malaysian Immigration Department for this year,” he said, adding that Johor tops the list with 29,832 applications over the past two years.

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: He stressed that there were more applicants in 2024 compared with 2025 in three states, except for Pahang, which showed a higher number in 2025 with 4,187 applications, up from 1,845 applications in 2024.

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: Sigit said Malaysia has always been a favourite destination for Indonesians to seek employment because of its ample jobs and a high minimum wage policy of RM1,700 (7.4 million rupiah).

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: “Currently, Jakarta is the region with the highest minimum wage in our country at five million rupiah (RM1,100).

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: “Furthermore, with some domestic economic issues, many Indonesians still prefer to head to Malaysia for employment.

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: “We at the consulate always remind our citizens to abide by Malaysian immigration laws, to come and work here legally and to never overstay,” he said in an interview.

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: Asked whether there was a sudden influx of requests to return home in the last few weeks of the amnesty programme, he said there was none.

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: “We typically issue a temporary passport within two days, provided that all their documents are in order,” he said.

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: He said that under the amnesty programme, an illegal immigrant is required to pay only RM520 before departing the country, a big reduction from the previous RM3,100 fine.

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: He said the consulate always reminds its citizens that those caught working illegally in Malaysia after the amnesty prog­ramme face imprisonment.

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: Immigration director-general Datuk Zakaria Saaban recently announced that the repatriation programme, which started on May 19 last year, has seen 228,961 foreigners sign up so far.

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: “Of the total, 204,523 have been repatriated and some RM114.5mil in fees has been collected,” Zakaria said.

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: An Indonesian known as Suharto, 42, said the amnesty programme allowed him to pay lower fines so that he could go home.

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: “I have been in Malaysia for many years.

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: It is time for me to return to Java to take care of my sickly parents,” he said, adding that the fuel crisis had impacted his employer’s construction business.

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: Another Indonesian, Elliza, 58, who has worked in Malaysia for nearly 35 years, expressed her concerns that the ongoing Middle East conflict may lead to more difficulties for those employed here.

title: Job scarcity and conflict driving more Indonesians home
text: “The economy in Indonesia is bad, but I just want to go home and be with my children,” she said.

title: MACC to continue questioning Rafizi’s former aide for a third day
text: MACC to continue questioning Rafizi’s former aide for a third day

title: MACC to continue questioning Rafizi’s former aide for a third day
text: PETALING JAYA: A former aide of ex-economy minister Datuk Seri Rafizi Ramli will continue to give his statement today to graft busters over a case involving a semiconductor project worth about RM1.1bil.

title: MACC to continue questioning Rafizi’s former aide for a third day
text: According to sources, James Chai, whose full name is Chai Jin Shern, was present at the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) headquarters in Putrajaya for a second day yesterday to continue providing information into the probe.

title: MACC to continue questioning Rafizi’s former aide for a third day
text: “The witness arrived at the MACC headquarters at about 10am for the second day of recording of his statement,” a source said.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: PHNOM PENH: While online scams have been reported to generate between US$12.5 billion and US$19 billion annually in the Kingdom, the language used has caused confusion.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: While the figure quoted is equivalent to as much as 40–60% of the Kingdom’s total GDP of US$51 billion, some have claimed that the figure is literally part of the Kingdom’s GDP.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: One example, the US-published “Transnational Crime in Southeast Asia” report, produced in May last year, stated that the scam industry in Cambodia made $12.5 billion, which is “equivalent” to approximately 40% of Cambodia’s total GDP.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: While “equivalent” is a term used to compare value to the GDP — with no part of scam income included in the GDP — some people have interpreted it as being a part of the GDP, causing confusion.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: A high-profile example of this misunderstanding was former Fox News host Jeanine Pirro, now US attorney for the District of Columbia.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: “Cambodia remains a source of focus, and public reporting suggests that 40 to 60% of Cambodia’s GDP is derived from scam revenue.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: While the Cambodian government recently appears to be taking more action against compounds, we are waiting to see whether that will actually change the prevalence of scams in Cambodia,” she said, responding to a question from The Post during an April 24 virtual press conference.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: Economists explained that Pirro’s remark reflected a flawed understanding of economics.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: For obvious reasons, scam earnings are not found in official statistics.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: “By definition, GDP is the market value of all final goods and services produced within a country’s borders during a specific period.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: As in other countries around the world, GDP only includes legal economic activities.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: It is not accurate to include illegal activities in the GDP because, by nature, they are difficult to verify, exist outside formal reporting systems, and are not reliable sources of information,” noted economist Duch Darin.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: “Formally, Cambodia’s GDP growth is derived from manufacturing, agriculture, tourism, construction, logistics and digital services, based on accepted international standards,” he added.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: Hong Vannak, an economist at the Royal Academy of Cambodia, echoed Darin’s sentiments, stating there is no room for illegal money in a country’s GDP.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: The figures can only be compared, not included.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: He noted that while there may be a gray area where a small part of scam revenue may end up circulating inside the legal economy, no economist can make an exact separation.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: “Scammers’ activities are hidden with no clear location, time or targets.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: Of course, they need to eat, travel and find accommodation, and they sometimes transfer some of their money to family.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: So, through food, travel and accommodation, this portion falls into daily expenses within a country.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: We can only say that this kind of money becomes a part of the GDP in that sense.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: “What has been reported is a comparison of scammers’ earnings to the total GDP.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: It is not that Cambodia’s GDP has a 40% share of scamming profits.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: Scamming doesn’t come from Cambodia only, but from many countries, such as Myanmar, Thailand and even some developed nations.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: Scamming takes place everywhere when states are loose in their control,” Vannak said.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: Cambodian economist Lor Vichet said that there is no clear study to indicate that a particular percentage of Cambodia’s GDP comes from scamming.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: Therefore, it remains a gray area.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: Only specific studies by expert institutions like the World Bank, IMF, ADB or the Ministry of Economy and Finance can make it clear, he suggested.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: In March, Prime Minister Hun Manet clarified that scams, which he labeled the “black economy,” have no place in Cambodia’s “white economy”.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: Those who benefit indirectly, such as drivers or landlords, were likened to a “gray economy”.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: The prime minister stressed that Cambodia’s general economy, such as tourism, is unrelated to scams, and rejected the accusation that 60 per cent of Cambodia’s total economy is from scams, calling such claims untrue.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: “Income from the black economy does not enter Cambodia’s official economy.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: It might go into house rentals, land purchases or some drivers’ salaries, but it does not contribute as a share of GDP — I want to be clear on that,” he said.

title: Economists explain myth of Cambodia’s scam income relationship to GDP
text: - The Phnom Penh Post/ANN

title: The 'Potential 2026' programme helps entrepreneurs survive under economic pressure and current challenging atmosphere
text: The 'Potential 2026' programme helps entrepreneurs survive under economic pressure and current challenging atmosphere

title: The 'Potential 2026' programme helps entrepreneurs survive under economic pressure and current challenging atmosphere
text: KUALA LUMPUR: In an increasingly challenging economic environment, a new self-development initiative has been introduced to help entrepreneurs enhance their resilience and business management capabilities.

title: The 'Potential 2026' programme helps entrepreneurs survive under economic pressure and current challenging atmosphere
text: The program known as "Potential 2026: Recognise & Master Your Personal, Financial, and Business Potential" was launched by entrepreneur mentor, Datuk Wira Dr. Azizan Osman, in collaboration with RichWorks as an effort to empower the local entrepreneurial community.

title: The 'Potential 2026' programme helps entrepreneurs survive under economic pressure and current challenging atmosphere
text: The initiative scheduled to take place this May focuses on the internal development of entrepreneurs, including aspects of mindset, finance, and business strategy to better prepare them for the current economic uncertainties.

title: The 'Potential 2026' programme helps entrepreneurs survive under economic pressure and current challenging atmosphere
text: According to the organisers, many entrepreneurs are currently facing pressure in terms of operational costs, cash flow, and rapid market changes.

title: The 'Potential 2026' programme helps entrepreneurs survive under economic pressure and current challenging atmosphere
text: Therefore, this program is designed to help participants identify their potential and subsequently leverage it to strengthen their respective businesses.

title: The 'Potential 2026' programme helps entrepreneurs survive under economic pressure and current challenging atmosphere
text: The approach taken is not merely theoretical; instead, it emphasises practical actions and mindset changes so that entrepreneurs can adapt to uncertain economic situations.

title: The 'Potential 2026' programme helps entrepreneurs survive under economic pressure and current challenging atmosphere
text: In addition, this program also aims to build confidence and mental resilience among entrepreneurs so that they do not easily give up when facing business challenges.

title: The 'Potential 2026' programme helps entrepreneurs survive under economic pressure and current challenging atmosphere
text: "Potential 2026" is seen as a continuation of various entrepreneurial development initiatives that have been introduced previously, with improvements aligned with current needs and global economic challenges.

title: The 'Potential 2026' programme helps entrepreneurs survive under economic pressure and current challenging atmosphere
text: Through this continuous effort, it is hoped that more local entrepreneurs will be able to rise, remain relevant, and continue to grow despite facing increasingly complex economic pressures.

title: The 'Potential 2026' programme helps entrepreneurs survive under economic pressure and current challenging atmosphere
text: Meanwhile, RichWorks Group Holdings Berhad (Richworks) continues to achieve remarkable milestones by receiving recognition from the Malaysia Book of Records (MBR) for its success in becoming the largest provider of SME entrepreneurship mentoring programs in the country.

title: The 'Potential 2026' programme helps entrepreneurs survive under economic pressure and current challenging atmosphere
text: The recognition was given after 4,302 entrepreneurs under the guidance of RichWorks from 2021 to February 2026, who generated a total sales of RM15.1 billion (2021-2025), thereby proving the effectiveness of the entrepreneurship development program implemented by the company.

title: The 'Potential 2026' programme helps entrepreneurs survive under economic pressure and current challenging atmosphere
text: Datuk Wira Dr Azizan Osman said that the achievement is not just a number, but reflects a comprehensive change experienced by entrepreneurs in terms of mindset, leadership, and business strategy.

title: The 'Potential 2026' programme helps entrepreneurs survive under economic pressure and current challenging atmosphere
text: According to him, success was born from the consistent actions of entrepreneurs who followed the guidance and their determination to grow in a challenging economic environment.

title: The 'Potential 2026' programme helps entrepreneurs survive under economic pressure and current challenging atmosphere
text: The mentoring initiative run by RichWorks focuses on the internal development of entrepreneurs, in addition to strengthening the aspects of financial management, marketing, and business operations comprehensively.

title: The 'Potential 2026' programme helps entrepreneurs survive under economic pressure and current challenging atmosphere
text: Moreover, the holistic approach used also helps entrepreneurs enhance their resilience, especially when facing market uncertainties and current economic pressures.

title: Money isn't everything: High-income S'gor ranks 14th for happiness
text: Money isn't everything: High-income S'gor ranks 14th for happiness

title: Money isn't everything: High-income S'gor ranks 14th for happiness
text: SHAH ALAM: Selangor ranks 14th out of Malaysia’s 16 states and federal territories in the latest national happiness index, trailing behind most of the country despite its economic dominance.

title: Money isn't everything: High-income S'gor ranks 14th for happiness
text: Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari said that while the state sits near the bottom of the list, it remains ahead of Kuala Lumpur and Penang.

title: Money isn't everything: High-income S'gor ranks 14th for happiness
text: He argued that the low rankings of Malaysia’s three highest-earning regions prove that wealth is not a definitive gauge of well-being.

title: Money isn't everything: High-income S'gor ranks 14th for happiness
text: “There are 13 indicators set by the Department of Statistics Malaysia (DOSM) that measure aspects of happiness,” Amirudin said in response to a question from Mohd Shafie Ngah (PN–Sungai Ramal).

title: Money isn't everything: High-income S'gor ranks 14th for happiness
text: “Among the highest scores for Selangor are family, religion, education, housing, and the environment,” the Mentri Besar said during the Selangor state assembly sitting in Bangunan Dewan Negeri Selangor in Shah Alam today (April 29).

title: Money isn't everything: High-income S'gor ranks 14th for happiness
text: Despite the overall state ranking, Amirudin highlighted that Ampang, Petaling Jaya, and Klang are officially recognised as the happiest areas under local authority management.

title: Money isn't everything: High-income S'gor ranks 14th for happiness
text: To lift the state’s standing, the government is targeting key stressors, including housing costs, public transport, and poverty.

title: Money isn't everything: High-income S'gor ranks 14th for happiness
text: Amirudin detailed an aggressive housing strategy to ease the burden on residents.

title: Money isn't everything: High-income S'gor ranks 14th for happiness
text: “We have launched housing projects amounting to 170,000 units, of which 40,000 are in various stages of development through our subsidiaries,” he said.

title: Money isn't everything: High-income S'gor ranks 14th for happiness
text:
“I believe more than 15,000 affordable homes have already been provided to residents, and we have also introduced the Smart Sewa Scheme to assist renters.”

Addressing frustrations over the public transport in the state, Amirudin pointed to the Smart Selangor Bus programme as a vital lifeline for many commuters.

title: Money isn't everything: High-income S'gor ranks 14th for happiness
text: He added that the state is now exploring Demand-Responsive Transit (DRT) to improve “last-mile” connectivity, though he admitted its effectiveness still requires refinement.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: BEIJING: When durian season arrives, it makes a grand entrance to China via railway wagon, cold-chain container and express vessel, bringing with it a fruit whose distinctive aroma may divide opinion, but whose commercial appeal is unmistakable.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: In the space of about 24 hours from the morning of April 25, three "durian express" vessels carrying 356 containers and more than 6,300 tonnes of fresh Thai durians arrived at Nansha Port in Guangzhou, south China's Guangdong Province.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: The fruit will be distributed across China in time to add to supplies for the upcoming May Day holiday.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: Since April 15, Nansha Customs has supervised the import of more than 9,500 tonnes of fresh durians.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: The railway picture is just as telling.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: From Jan 1 to April 26, the China-Laos Railway transported 50,300 tonnes of imported durians, up 94.2 per cent year on year, according to China Railway Kunming Group.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: The figures show not only that Chinese consumers are buying more durians, but also that the supply routes serving this growing demand are becoming faster and more efficient.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: Durian has long occupied an unusual place in China's fruit market.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: For devoted buyers, it is the "king of fruits," rich, creamy and worth paying for.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: For others, its powerful smell remains a barrier.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: Yet that traditional divide is narrowing as durians move beyond boutique supermarkets and first-tier urban consumers.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: Better cold-chain logistics, more efficient cross-border supply chains and the spread of e-commerce have brought durians into community group-buying channels, lower-tier cities and county-level markets.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: That expansion has made China the center of gravity for the global durian trade.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: Customs data show that China imported about 1.87 million tonnes of fresh durians in 2025, making it the world's most important consumer market for the fruit.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: Industry participants say the consumer base has grown rapidly.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: Rising incomes, larger supply and improved cold-chain infrastructure have all helped enlarge the market.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: For ports and railways, the challenge is speed, as fresh fruit is an unforgiving cargo.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: At Mohan railway port, the core hub for China-Laos Railway international freight, authorities have deepened coordination with Laos' Boten port, streamlined customs procedures and worked to keep cross-border logistics flowing.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: Railway departments, customs and companies have formed special teams for fresh-fruit transport.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: Green channels for inspection give priority to entry, inspection and testing, linking arrival, clearance and transfer more smoothly and sharply reducing the time fruit spends at the port.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: Nansha, China's largest seaborne port for durian imports, is making a similar effort.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: As Southeast Asia's main producing areas enter peak season, its durian imports are expected to keep rising between April and June.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: Yet scale has not reduced scrutiny.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: For the concentrated arrivals in late April, customs officers carried out targeted inspection and quarantine, checking for harmful organisms and taking samples for pesticide residues, heavy metals and other safety indicators.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: "Visible pest risks can be checked by the eye, but invisible microbes and contaminants must also be strictly guarded against," said Lin Xiaojing, an official with Nansha Customs.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: As a result, the market is expanding not only in size but also in standards.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: Niti Pratoomvongsa, commercial counsellor at the Royal Thai Consulate-General in Nanning, capital of south China's Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region, said Chinese consumers are shifting from a focus on price toward a stronger concern for quality, food safety, production standards and traceability.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: This change is raising the bar for competition.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: South-East Asian suppliers are responding.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: As planting areas expand in Thailand, Vietnam, Malaysia and Cambodia, industry participants say the competition among South-East Asian suppliers in China is still being reshaped.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: Chinese capital and business operators are also moving further upstream.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: Liu Junhong, a Guangxi businessman who shifted from engineering and agricultural irrigation into durians five years ago, has inspected markets in Malaysia, Thailand and Vietnam and invested in a planting project in Cambodia's Kampot Province.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: "The industry is still expanding rapidly, but already entering a period of structural differentiation," Liu said.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: His answer is to focus on higher-end varieties such as "Black Thorn" to avoid future homogenized competition.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: Growth, however, does not guarantee easy profits for everyone.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: Wang Zhengbo, chairman of Guangxi TWT Supply Chain Management, a long-time trading company specializing in Thai durian, noted that the durian chain is long and layered, from planting to retail, with costs accumulating at each stage.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: Large traders and those controlling upstream resources are relatively stable, while some smaller players are more exposed to price volatility.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: At the production end, Nicholas Lui, a durian grower from Malaysia's Pahang state, said rising fertilizer, fuel and labor costs, together with stricter procurement standards, are narrowing margins.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: Even so, the direction of travel remains clear.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: China's market is widely seen by industry participants as far from saturated, but it is becoming more selective.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: Poor-quality fruit, uneven standards and inefficient supply chains will find it harder to survive.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: The boom is also creating new forms of consumption.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: In Nansha, a port-side fresh market model allows fruit to be unloaded, containers opened and products sold directly.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: A durian carnival scheduled for April 30 will offer Thai Monthong, Malaysian Musang King and other varieties to consumers.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: In producing countries, some businesses are exploring durian tourism, combining picking experiences with agricultural travel.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: Processed products, from frozen pulp to cakes and snacks, are also extending the value chain.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: The fruit's flavor may not have changed.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: But from orchard to port, railway and dinner table, the business around it is becoming faster, stricter and more regional.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: For China and Southeast Asia, the durian season is no longer just a seasonal rush.

title: China's durian boom is ripening into a regional supply-chain story: Economic Watch
text: It is a vivid example of how consumer demand can reshape the routes, standards and economics of a whole supply chain.- Xinhua

title: Property market may still need policy easing
text: Property market may still need policy easing

title: Property market may still need policy easing
text: While China's housing market has shown tentative recovery signals, a sustained rebound still hinges on continued policy easing, analysts and policy advisers said, with further scope remaining for interest rate reductions and purchase restriction removals.

title: Property market may still need policy easing
text: Their comments come as the sector is closely watching policymakers' property market policy stances, as improving property market data have reignited debate over whether China's five-year-long housing market correction is approaching a turning point.

title: Property market may still need policy easing
text: Zhang Yu, managing director and chief real estate analyst at China International Capital Corp, said the recent pickup in home sales in Beijing and Shanghai reflects improving supply-demand dynamics.

title: Property market may still need policy easing
text: "In Beijing and Shanghai, listings in the existing home market have begun to decline while transaction volumes have stabilised, suggesting a shortening absorption cycle — a key indicator of how quickly available housing inventory can be cleared — and offering positive signals of market stabilisation," Zhang said.

title: Property market may still need policy easing
text: The National Bureau of Statistics said secondhand home prices in China's first-tier cities — Beijing and Shanghai, as well as Guangzhou and Shenzhen in Guangdong province — climbed 0.4 percent in March on a monthly basis, reversing a 0.1 percent decline in February.

title: Property market may still need policy easing
text: In Beijing, secondhand home prices rose 0.6 percent in March, while Shanghai saw a 0.4 percent increase.

title: Property market may still need policy easing
text: Home prices and transaction volumes in these two cities are closely followed by market observers as a barometer of the broader property market.

title: Property market may still need policy easing
text: Most of the transactions were concentrated in homes priced below 3 million yuan ($438,876), Zhang said, indicating demand remains heavily skewed toward first-time buyers, cautioning that without stronger participation from buyers who need to improve their homes, the recovery could lack sustainability.

title: Property market may still need policy easing
text: Both improvements in macroeconomic conditions and the continuity of property market policy support are essential to stimulate upgrade demand and extend the nascent property market recovery into more cities, he said, and suggested further easing home purchase restrictions, lowering mortgage rates, facilitating mortgage transfers between buyers and sellers, and offering tax deductions tied to housing consumption.

title: Property market may still need policy easing
text: Zhang also emphasised the need to develop institution-led rental housing and lower thresholds for real estate investment trusts to facilitate a meaningful expansion in REIT issuances to support rental housing.

title: Property market may still need policy easing
text: Zhang Bin, a nonresident senior research fellow at the China Finance 40 Forum, said further stabilising the property sector not only requires sector-specific policies, but also broader efforts to address insufficient domestic demand and foster a recovery in inflation.

title: Property market may still need policy easing
text: Property valuations can be deemed as the discounted value of future rental incomes, said Zhang, who is also deputy director of the Institute of World Economics and Politics at the Chinese Academy of Social Sciences and a national political adviser.

title: Property market may still need policy easing
text: Therefore, a key policy lever to stabilise property prices would be further interest rate cuts, Zhang said, as they would help lift inflation expectations, support rental outlooks and — by lowering discount rates and improving risk appetite — bolster property valuations.

title: Property market may still need policy easing
text: Pan Gongsheng, governor of the People's Bank of China, the country's central bank, said last month that it will flexibly and effectively employ a range of policy instruments, including interest rate cuts, to promote steady economic growth and a reasonable recovery in overall price levels.

title: Property market may still need policy easing
text: - China DailyANN

title: Malaysia's inflation eases to 1.4% in 2025 - DOSM
text: Malaysia's inflation eases to 1.4% in 2025 - DOSM

title: Malaysia's inflation eases to 1.4% in 2025 - DOSM
text: KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia’s inflation moderated to 1.4 per cent in 2025 as compared to 1.8 per cent in the previous year, supported by the firmer ringgit, sustained household spending and initiatives by the government.

title: Malaysia's inflation eases to 1.4% in 2025 - DOSM
text: According to the Department of Statistics Malaysia (DOSM), government initiatives to distribute RON95 petrol subsidies to Malaysians, to a certain extent, helped to curb further increases in Malaysia’s inflation.

title: Malaysia's inflation eases to 1.4% in 2025 - DOSM
text: "This rate is within the inflation range projected by Bank Negara Malaysia and the Ministry of Finance, between 1.0 per cent and 2.0 per cent for 2025,” it said in a statement regarding the analysis of Consumer Price Index (CPI) 2025.

title: Malaysia's inflation eases to 1.4% in 2025 - DOSM
text: Chief statistician Datuk Seri Dr Mohd Uzir Mahidin said the country’s inflation in 2025 was contributed by the inflation of housing, water, electricity, gas and other fuels (1.6 per cent); health (1.2 per cent); recreation, sports and culture (1.1 per cent); transport (0.4 per cent) and furnishings, household equipment and routine household maintenance (0.2 per cent).

title: Malaysia's inflation eases to 1.4% in 2025 - DOSM
text: He said personal care, social protection and miscellaneous goods and services recorded a higher increase at 4.4 per cent compared to 2024's 3.0 per cent, followed by insurance and financial services (3.4 per cent), restaurants and accommodation services (3.2 per cent), education (2.3 per cent), and food and beverages (2.1 per cent).

title: Malaysia's inflation eases to 1.4% in 2025 - DOSM
text: In contrast, he said information and communication and clothing and footwear recorded declines of negative 4.3 per cent and negative 0.2 per cent in 2025, respectively.

title: Malaysia's inflation eases to 1.4% in 2025 - DOSM
text: "The highest inflation throughout 2025 was recorded in January 2025 at 1.7 per cent and edged down to 1.1 per cent in June 2025, contributed by a slower trend in the inflation of food and beverages, and housing, water, electricity, gas and other fuels.

title: Malaysia's inflation eases to 1.4% in 2025 - DOSM
text: "Nevertheless, the inflation trend picked up in the second half of the year 2025,” he said.

title: Malaysia's inflation eases to 1.4% in 2025 - DOSM
text: Mohd Uzir noted that the core inflation rose 2.0 per cent in 2025 as compared to 1.8 per cent in 2024, contributed by the personal care, social protection and miscellaneous goods and services (4.4 per cent), food and beverages (3.4 per cent), insurance and financial services (3.4 per cent), and restaurant and accommodation services (3.2 per cent).

title: Malaysia's inflation eases to 1.4% in 2025 - DOSM
text: Meanwhile, he said most states registered slower inflation, with four states exceeding the national inflation rate of 1.4 per cent in 2025, namely Johor (2.0 per cent), Selangor (1.7 per cent), Negeri Sembilan (1.7 per cent) and Melaka (1.5 per cent).

title: Malaysia's inflation eases to 1.4% in 2025 - DOSM
text: "Kelantan recorded the lowest increase at 0.3 per cent,” he said.

title: Malaysia's inflation eases to 1.4% in 2025 - DOSM
text: Mohd Uzir said that, compared with inflation in other selected countries, the inflation rate in ASEAN countries ranged from negative 0.3 per cent to 7.7 per cent in 2025.

title: Malaysia's inflation eases to 1.4% in 2025 - DOSM
text: "Laos recorded the highest inflation at 7.7 per cent, while Brunei recorded the lowest inflation at negative 0.3 per cent,” he added.

title: Malaysia's inflation eases to 1.4% in 2025 - DOSM

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: THE rapid development of artificial intelligence (AI) has revived a classical debate in economics regarding technological unemployment.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: Will AI innovation bring shared prosperity, or create an unprecedented unemployment crisis?

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: This is not merely a theoretical inquiry for academic journals.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: Current AI systems are already demonstrating a capacity for complex reasoning that rivals human experts in high-stakes fields such as business strategy, legal analysis and sophisticated software engineering.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: According to the McKinsey Global Institute, the scale of this shift is staggering, with estimates suggesting that up to 375 million workers globally – roughly 14% of the global workforce – will need to transition into entirely new occupational categories by 2030.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: As this inevitable force of economic disruption gains momentum, it is imperative to critically assess Indonesia’s strategic preparedness.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: While Indonesia’s current policy framework signals a robust commitment to establishing an AI-driven economy, there is a glaring disparity in its focus.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: The necessary mitigative measures designed to address the undesirable socio-economic consequences of AI remain dangerously underdeveloped.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: At the heart of the nation’s strategy is the proposed Presidential Decree of the National Artificial Intelligence Road Map 2026–2029.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: This document is intended to serve as the definitive anchor for Indonesia’s technological trajectory over the next four years, centreing on four primary pillars: strengthening stakeholder involvement, fostering domestic innovation, increasing research capabilities and mitigating systemic risks.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: However, a closer look at the road map’s 45 detailed programmes reveals a lopsided priority list.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: Approximately 90% of these initiatives are aggressively directed toward “offensive” strategies – preparing AI talent, building innovation ecosystems, expanding physical infrastructure and courting international investment.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: In contrast, only five programmes are dedicated to the “defensive” side of the equation: the social and economic risks of displacement.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: This composition reveals a single-minded objective: the rapid acceleration of AI adoption at any cost.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: While proactive adoption is necessary to maintain Indonesia’s global competitiveness, an unmanaged transition will inevitably produce adverse side effects.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: If left unmitigated, technological unemployment will not only widen the wealth gap but also risk fracturing the nation’s social and political stability.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: The omissions in the current road map are not insignificant; they represent a critical structural gap.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: As it stands, the document includes no mention of reforming Indonesia’s existing unemployment benefit schemes (such as the Job Loss Insurance programme) to cover the unique challenges of AI-driven job displacement.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: Furthermore, there are no specific provisions for the informal sector – a massive segment of the population that constitutes 59.4% of the national workforce and lacks the safety nets afforded to formal employees.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: Without dedicated funding mechanisms to bridge the income gap during the “Great Transition”, the promise of AI could quickly turn into a source of national precarity.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: Historically, the fear of “the machine” is a recurring anxiety, dating back to the era of David Ricardo and the Luddite protests.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: In past technological revolutions, the “optimal solution” for absorbing shocks was consistently found in education.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: When manual labour was displaced during the Industrial Revolution, the resulting demand for cognitive and clerical skills was met by a massive expansion in public schooling.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: This pattern has largely held true for over a century; as old roles become obsolete, new and once-unimaginable vocations emerge.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: As noted by economist David Autor, roughly 60% of the jobs held by American workers in 2018 simply did not exist in 1940.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: Yet, relying on historical precedent may be a dangerous form of survivorship bias.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: The unprecedented speed of AI development and its ability to “learn” at an exponential rate suggests that this wave of disruption is fundamentally different from those of the past.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: In this new context, traditional reskilling and retraining programmes may hit a ceiling.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: When AI can be deployed instantly across an entire sector, the “absorption capacity” of the labour market is stretched to its breaking point.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: If firms adopt AI to achieve massive gains in efficiency with a fraction of the human capital, entry-level positions involving repetitive cognitive tasks will be the first to vanish.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: This is the precise area where Indonesia – already struggling to generate a sufficient volume of high-quality, decent jobs for its youth – is most vulnerable.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: The honest, albeit uncomfortable, answer is that it is highly improbable for Indonesia to generate new, high-value jobs at a pace that matches the speed of AI displacement.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: Even in a scenario where new roles are created, the question of distribution looms large.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: Competition for the few available AI-enabled roles will be fierce, particularly if the majority of the workforce is pulling from the same standardised government training programmes.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text: Without significant skill differentiation, workers will likely face stagnant wages and increased workloads as they compete for a shrinking pool of middle-class roles.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow
text:
— The Jakarta Post /ANN

Baginda Muda Bangsa is a political economy analyst at Laboratorium Indonesia 2045.

title: Indonesia’s AI strategy: Economically ambitious, socially hollow

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: Responding constructively to outside warnings

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: THE memory of the November 1997 economic crisis still carries weight in South Korea.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: Faced with the risk of sovereign default, the country turned to a massive bailout led by the International Monetary Fund (IMF) after having failed to push through painful domestic reforms in earlier years.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: While it is often recorded simply as one of many turbulent incidents or accidents, the warning signs had been building for quite some time.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: In the months before the bailout request, financial instability was already spreading across East Asia, and a series of warnings pointed to South Korea’s structural weaknesses and urged caution.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: At home, key indicators – including the current account balance, the won’s exchange rate, stock prices and broader measures of economic sentiment – were all flashing signals of unease.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text:
In denial

Yet policymakers at the time did not respond with sufficient caution.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: Instead, they tended to dismiss outside warnings as biased or ill-intentioned.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: Some even framed them as deliberate attacks.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: Senior officials, including top economic aides to the president and the finance minister at the time, publicly insisted that South Korea’s economic fundamentals were sound and that there was little cause for concern.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: The chief presidential aide on economic policy emphasised even a month before the bailout request that all the warning calls were ill-founded and that the country’s economic fundamentals were strong.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: South Korea was eventually able to recover from the crisis much faster than most had expected, repaying IMF loans ahead of schedule, carrying out restructuring and regaining growth momentum to join the ranks of major economies.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: In hindsight, some of the official statements at the time may have been intended to calm markets and prevent panic.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: Nearly three decades later, it is worrying to see a familiar pattern reemerge as a recent IMF Fiscal Monitor report has drawn renewed attention to South Korea’s fiscal path, putting the government’s response under scrutiny as much as the numbers themselves.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: The fund said in its April report that South Korea is likely to face a significant rise in its government debt ratio in the coming years.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: The fund noted some improvement compared with its previous outlook, but still singled out South Korea, along with Belgium, as a country where the pace of debt increase stands out.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: It projected that South Korea’s general government debt-to-gross domestic product ratio, known as D2, will rise rapidly to exceed 60% within three years and reach around 63% by 2031.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: This D2 measure includes not only central and local government debt but also liabilities of non-profit public institutions, allowing for cross-country comparisons.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: The IMF publishes such assessments twice a year based on its regular reviews of fiscal conditions, meaning the April report was not an ad hoc event.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: South Korea’s absolute debt level is not yet at a point that signals an imminent crisis, but what stands out is the pace of increase, which has been among the fastest in the world in recent years.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: Covid-19 pandemic-related spending explains part of the rise, but so does the broader difficulty of restoring stronger growth momentum, which serves as the denominator.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: The more immediate controversy, however, lies in how the government reacted to the widely accepted and regularly published report.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: Senior officials at the presidential office dismissed the IMF assessment as overly alarmist, using expressions that suggested the report was designed to provoke fear.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: The budget minister also pushed back, describing the outlook as excessive.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: Their argument is not without merit, as fiscal sustainability cannot be judged by headline debt figures alone and must take into account future growth prospects and medium-term fiscal conditions.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text:
Ageing population

At the same time, officials acknowledged underlying risks, including rapid population ageing and ongoing concerns about the sustainability of the national pension system.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: Still, the tone of the response is hard to ignore, as it does not replicate the events of 1997 but echoes them in the tendency to react defensively to external warnings rather than engage in a measured and constructive way.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: South Korea’s debt ratio has indeed been rising faster than that of many peers, a trend that inevitably raises questions about the outlook.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: There is an element of irony in the current debate, too, as many of today’s policymakers and ruling party figures were once vocal critics of rising government debt, warning that fiscal expansion could trigger a crisis and sharply attacking the government of the time.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: At that time, the debt ratio was below 40%, whereas now it is already as high as around 60%.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: It is reasonable to argue that what matters is not the level of debt itself but whether the economy can sustain it, since the debt ratio reflects both the size of liabilities and the scale of economic output for a year.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: If economic growth outpaces debt accumulation, the debt ratio can stabilise or even decline over time.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: But that is precisely where the debate should focus, as the issue is less about whether the IMF’s projections are perfectly accurate than about the selective way external assessments are received within the government and political community.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: Similar warnings were once embraced and used to criticise the government, yet now they are largely dismissed.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: If those earlier positions were politically motivated, that should be acknowledged, and if they were based on genuine concern, the same standard should apply today.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: In either case, consistency matters.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: More importantly, repeated references to growth potential must be backed by tangible results.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: For years, successive governments have struggled to lift South Korea’s underlying growth capacity, and acknowledging that record would be a more credible starting point than disputing projections.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: What is needed now is not a debate over tone, but a clear and practical plan to strengthen the economy’s long-term trajectory while encouraging constructive debate on the growth of government debt, which no one can deny is too fast.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text:
— The Korea Herald/ANN

Yoo Choon-sik worked for nearly 30 years at Reuters, including as chief South Korea economics correspondent, and briefly as a business strategy consultant.

title: Responding constructively to outside warnings
text: The views expressed here are the writer’s own.

title: Supply chain under strain
text: Supply chain under strain

title: Supply chain under strain
text: PETALING JAYA: Cost pressures are beginning to emerge in the domestic food supply chain, says Economy Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir.

title: Supply chain under strain
text: “In terms of cost of living, selected food prices remain within a controlled range, although input pressures are beginning to emerge.

title: Supply chain under strain
text: “Between April 20 and April 22, price movements for selected food items remained below 10%,” he said during the Global Supply Crisis briefing yesterday.

title: Supply chain under strain
text: He said the average price of standard chicken rose 3.3% to RM9.70 per kg, grade C eggs increased 7.3% to RM3.93 per 10 eggs, while beef prices declined 3.8% to RM35.92 per kg.

title: Supply chain under strain
text: For seafood, kembung fell 1.1% to RM16.10 per kg, while cencaru and white prawns/shrimps rose 0.95% to RM10.36 per kg and 0.61% to RM32.03 per kg, respectively.

title: Supply chain under strain
text: As for vegetables, mustard greens rose from RM6.16 to RM6.51 per kg, spinach increased nearly 5% to RM5.59 per kg and fresh coconut milk went up 3.6% to RM16.41 per kg.

title: Supply chain under strain
text:
“These trends indicate that input and logistics cost pressures are beginning to signal early stress within the domestic food supply chain.”

Akmal Nasrullah said the government will monitor and implement necessary measures to mitigate the impact on consumers and maintain price stability.

title: Supply chain under strain
text: This must extend beyond retail prices and cover upstream factors, including fertiliser, diesel, energy, raw materials, production, transportation and distribution, he said.

title: Supply chain under strain
text: He said the government is focusing on fertiliser supply and agricultural inputs to ensure food security, including promoting bio-organic fertilisers and circular-economy practices that use biomass and agricultural waste.

title: Supply chain under strain
text: A high-level biofuels committee has also been established to coordinate and accelerate the biodiesel agenda.

title: Supply chain under strain
text: “As the prices of fuels such as coal and gas increase, the cost of electricity generation also rises, potentially affecting consumer tariffs if not carefully managed,” he said.

title: Supply chain under strain
text: The Energy Commission is monitoring coal stock levels at power plants to ensure minimum supply requirements are maintained.

title: Supply chain under strain
text: Akmal Nasrullah said electricity demand is rising, largely driven by hot weather and higher air-conditioner use.

title: Supply chain under strain
text: However, he assured that 85% of households, or 7.5 million domestic users, consuming less than 600kWh will remain fully exempt from the Automatic Fuel Adjustment charge.

title: Supply chain under strain
text: “The construction sector is also experiencing the effects of rising fuel and material costs.

title: Supply chain under strain
text: Since late March 2026, the industry has reported cost pressures due to increases in diesel, bitumen, premix, logistics and building materials,” he said.

title: Supply chain under strain
text: Citing data from the Cons­truction Industry Development Board, Akmal Nasrullah said the average price increase for seven key materials, including bitumen, concrete, sand, aggregates, steel and bricks, has reached 12.59%.

title: Supply chain under strain
text: Building materials account for the largest share of construction costs at 64.2%.

title: Supply chain under strain
text: He said mitigation measures such as getting banks to offer loan rescheduling and restructuring options under a scheme to ease cash flow pressures, particularly for small contractors, will be implemented.

title: Supply chain under strain
text: He outlined five strategic priorities to ensure economic stability and the well-being of the people – stabilising energy and critical input supply, controlling cost-of-living pressures, ensuring key public projects are not stalled, protecting jobs and family incomes, and building long-term economic resilience.

title: Supply chain under strain
text: “Its impact has expanded to supply chains, logistics costs, energy supplies, industrial raw materials, agricultural inputs, food prices, the construction sector, labour market and investor confidence,” he said.

title: UAE leaves OPEC and OPEC+ in major blow to global oil producers' group
text: UAE leaves OPEC and OPEC+ in major blow to global oil producers' group

title: UAE leaves OPEC and OPEC+ in major blow to global oil producers' group
text: DUBAI, April 28 (Reuters) - ⁠The United Arab Emirates said on Tuesday it was quitting OPEC and OPEC+, dealing a heavy ⁠blow to the oil exporting groups and their de facto leader, Saudi Arabia, at ‌a time when the Iran war has caused a historic energy shock and unsettled the global economy.

title: UAE leaves OPEC and OPEC+ in major blow to global oil producers' group
text: The loss of the UAE, a longstanding OPEC member, could create disarray and weaken the group, which has usually sought to show a united front despite internal ​disagreements over a range of issues from geopolitics to production quotas.

title: UAE leaves OPEC and OPEC+ in major blow to global oil producers' group
text: UAE ⁠Energy Minister Suhail Mohamed al-Mazrouei told Reuters ⁠the decision was taken after a careful look at the regional power's energy strategies.

title: UAE leaves OPEC and OPEC+ in major blow to global oil producers' group
text: Asked whether the UAE consulted ⁠with ‌Saudi Arabia, he said the UAE did not raise the issue with any other country.

title: UAE leaves OPEC and OPEC+ in major blow to global oil producers' group
text: "This is a policy decision, it has been done after a careful look at current and future policies ⁠related to level of production," said the energy minister.

title: UAE leaves OPEC and OPEC+ in major blow to global oil producers' group
text: OPEC Gulf producers ​have already been struggling to ‌ship exports through the Strait of Hormuz, a chokepoint between Iran and Oman through which a ⁠fifth of the ​world’s crude oil and liquefied natural gas normally passes, because of Iranian threats and attacks against vessels.

title: UAE leaves OPEC and OPEC+ in major blow to global oil producers' group
text: Mazrouei said the move would not have a huge impact on the market because of the situation in the strait.

title: UAE leaves OPEC and OPEC+ in major blow to global oil producers' group
text: But the UAE exit ⁠from OPEC represents a win for U.S. President Donald Trump, ​who has accused the organisation of "ripping off the rest of the world" by inflating oil prices.

title: UAE leaves OPEC and OPEC+ in major blow to global oil producers' group
text: Trump has also linked U.S. military support for the Gulf with oil prices, saying that while the U.S. defends OPEC members they "exploit ⁠this by imposing high oil prices".

title: UAE leaves OPEC and OPEC+ in major blow to global oil producers' group
text: The move came after the UAE, a regional business hub and one of Washington's most important allies, criticised fellow Arab states for not doing enough to protect it from numerous Iranian attacks during the war.

title: UAE leaves OPEC and OPEC+ in major blow to global oil producers' group
text: Anwar Gargash, the diplomatic adviser for the UAE president, criticised the Arab and Gulf response ​to the Iranian attacks in a session at the Gulf Influencers Forum ⁠on Monday.

title: UAE leaves OPEC and OPEC+ in major blow to global oil producers' group
text: "The Gulf Cooperation Council countries supported each other logistically, but politically and militarily, I think their position ​has been the weakest historically," Gargash said.

title: UAE leaves OPEC and OPEC+ in major blow to global oil producers' group
text: "I expect this weak stance ‌from the Arab League and I am not surprised ​by it, but I haven't expected it from the (Gulf) Cooperation Council and I am surprised by it," he said.

title: UAE leaves OPEC and OPEC+ in major blow to global oil producers' group
text: (Additional reporting by Nayera Abdallah; Writing by Michael Georgy; Editing by Aidan Lewis)

title: Local equity market remains resilient, reflects investor confidence, says Akmal Nasrullah
text: Local equity market remains resilient, reflects investor confidence, says Akmal Nasrullah

title: Local equity market remains resilient, reflects investor confidence, says Akmal Nasrullah
text: KUALA LUMPUR: The local equity market remained resilient, rising 1.5 per cent last Friday, reflecting investor confidence in the country’s economic fundamentals, Economy Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir said.

title: Local equity market remains resilient, reflects investor confidence, says Akmal Nasrullah
text: The benchmark index, the FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI (FBM KLCI), rose to 1,720.3 points on April 24, 2026, compared with 1,695.21 points the previous week, he said during an online briefing on the global supply crisis today.

title: Local equity market remains resilient, reflects investor confidence, says Akmal Nasrullah
text: "The index also reached a high of 1,721.7 points on April 23, 2026, surpassing the pre-conflict level of 1,716.61 points recorded before Feb 27, 2026.

title: Local equity market remains resilient, reflects investor confidence, says Akmal Nasrullah
text: "This reflects investor confidence in the country’s economic fundamentals.

title: Local equity market remains resilient, reflects investor confidence, says Akmal Nasrullah
text: However, the government does not assess economic strength solely based on financial markets,” he said.

title: Local equity market remains resilient, reflects investor confidence, says Akmal Nasrullah
text: Akmal Nasrullah said true economic strength must also be measured by households’ ability to manage daily expenses, businesses maintaining operations, workers retaining jobs, and industries continuing to receive necessary inputs.

title: Local equity market remains resilient, reflects investor confidence, says Akmal Nasrullah
text: He added that government-linked companies (GLCs) and government-linked investment companies (GLICs) play a broader role beyond safeguarding investment returns, including strengthening economic resilience through investments in energy, logistics, food, technology, infrastructure and high-value-added industries.

title: Local equity market remains resilient, reflects investor confidence, says Akmal Nasrullah
text: The National Economic Action Council (MTEN) also heard a briefing from Khazanah Nasional Bhd on the medium-term economic implications of the global supply crisis, he said.

title: Local equity market remains resilient, reflects investor confidence, says Akmal Nasrullah
text: "Among the implications shared by Khazanah is that the global supply crisis is changing how companies and investors evaluate investment locations.

title: Local equity market remains resilient, reflects investor confidence, says Akmal Nasrullah
text: "Energy stability, access to input materials, logistics continuity and a country’s ability to manage shocks have become key factors in investment decisions,” he said.

title: Local equity market remains resilient, reflects investor confidence, says Akmal Nasrullah
text: He added that countries able to secure energy supply, safeguard industrial operations and strengthen domestic supply chains would have a stronger advantage in attracting investment.

title: Local equity market remains resilient, reflects investor confidence, says Akmal Nasrullah
text: Malaysia, he said, has several key structural strengths, including its strategic geographical position, diversified industrial base, stable energy infrastructure, as well as a maturing electrical and electronics and semiconductor ecosystem.
